instructions.instruction: Always confirm release constraints (date, budget, team size) before categorizing any requirements.
instructions.relevant_when: Starting a MoSCoW prioritization session or categorizing requirements for a release.
instructions.why_given: Categories are invalid without timeline and capacity context; plans built without constraints collapse during execution.

instructions.instruction: Apply the failure-focused test for every Must candidate: ask 'Does the release fail without this item?' — if not, it is at most Should.
instructions.relevant_when: Assigning the Must category to a requirement.
instructions.why_given: Stakeholder preference is not the same as release-critical need; inflated Must lists block true essentials.

instructions.instruction: Validate that Must items do not exceed 60% of total estimated effort. If Must exceeds 60%, rebalance or escalate to stakeholders.
instructions.relevant_when: After completing initial categorization, before finalizing the priority table.
instructions.why_given: Must work exceeding 60% of effort makes delivery failure likely; the 60% threshold is the skill's formal validation gate.

instructions.instruction: Always include explicit Won't items with a revisit date; never leave the backlog open-ended.
instructions.why_given: Missing Won't items invite silent scope creep and cause the team to repeatedly reopen settled decisions.

instructions.instruction: Record rationale (business outcome and risk) for every category assignment, not just the item and its tier.
instructions.relevant_when: Publishing or documenting a MoSCoW decision table.
instructions.why_given: Unjustified categories cannot be defended in reviews and get re-litigated repeatedly.
